That said, the existing databases (e.g. GlobalFungi, Global Soil Mycobiome, GenBank) have led to rapid progress in studying EcM fungal diversity at scale, and the numerous, recent contributions from the Society for the Protection of Underground Networks (SPUN; <https://www.spun.earth/>) and collaborators exemplify these efforts [e.g. @vetrovskyMetaanalysisGlobalFungal2019; @tedersooGlobalSoilMycobiome2021; @mikryukovConnectingMultipleDimensions2023; @vannulandClimateMismatchesEctomycorrhizal2024; @vannulandGlobalHotspotsMycorrhizal2025; @vangalenBiogeographyConservationEarths2025; @qinHighResolutionRangeMapping2026].

Finally, for some shortfalls like the Prestonian (abundance patterns) and Darwinian (evolutionary relationships), Canada is essentially starting from scratch. While daunting, this also opens the door for optimizing how we collect data from the beginning, learning from efforts made in other regions, and targeting the most pressing aspects or questions pertaining to the different data shortfalls. For example, while studies have explored shifts in EcM fungal abundance following specific disturbances like nitrogen addition [@hogberg2021carbon] or warming [@clemmensen2006increased], understanding how EcM fungi respond to fire is also critical in a region like Canada, where single fire seasons can produce burns greater in area than ~40% of European nations (in 2023, over 165 thousand km² of forest burned in Canada). Post-fire fungal community surveys often show a reduction in detectable EcM fungi, especially following high severity fire that kills EcM host trees [@dove2017fire; @pulidochavez2021highseverity; @packardSuccessionalChangesFungal2023]. But EcM fungi have been shown to persist, dormant in the soil, until new host seedlings begin to establish [@baar1999mycorrhizal; @yamaoka2025nitrogen]. Better understanding of EcM fungal community distribution, dynamics, dispersal, and persistence is therefore critical for supporting ecosystem recovery and management efforts.

Finally, while we are confident about the data and methods we used to evaluate the seven shortfalls, our study has some important limitations that warrant discussion. First, we based our evaluations exclusively on publicly available and discoverable DNA sequence data from GlobalFungi and GenBank, and did not consider other kinds of data including physical specimen occurrence records available through the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF). While taxonomic inconsistencies and cryptic diversity render such data less reliable for the present goals, they can nonetheless complement future, molecular-based sampling efforts.  For example, Figure S5 shows a map of physical specimen occurrence records, pulled from GBIF, for **`r nf(as.numeric(V(linn, "GBIF: EcM genera represented (genera with sequence data)")) + as.numeric(V(linn, "GBIF: EcM genera represented (genera without sequence data)")))`** EcM fungal genera, with records distinguished according to whether the genus is represented within our Canada-specific DNA sequence datasets. Table S4 shows the **`r nf(V(linn, "GBIF: EcM genera represented (genera without sequence data)"))`** genera that currently lack associated DNA sequence data in Canada. This list could be used to target future sampling efforts.  Second, our shortfall assessments were not informed in any systematic way by data available solely in the primary research literature; rather, we relied exclusively on curated, discoverable, and publicly accessible data repositories like Mycocosm, BioTime, and FungalRoot. Some shortfalls may therefore be less severe in reality than our limited data suggest. Third, like all studies of this kind, the data acquisition, processing, and analysis pipeline (including bioinformatics pipelines implemented by original sources) involves many decisions, some of which are less well-rationalized than others. This yields a "multiverse" of unique analysis pipelines whose outcomes could vary materially **[@steegenIncreasingTransparencyMultiverse2016]**.  Ideally, one would conduct a "multiverse analysis" that reports the results from all unique pipelines **[@shortSystematicMultiverseAnalysis2025]**.  We are currently exploring that option, but in the interim, for full transparency we provide a thorough "Decisions Log" in the Supplemental Information S2 that documents all decisions and their rationales. Lastly, the data presented in this study represent the shortfalls realized at a given point in time. We are in the process of developing a web-based dashboard that will update our shortfall analyses (plus complementary analyses) quarterly - a form of "digital twin" [@visserDigitalTwinsTool2026].

# Conclusion

While we are making progress in gathering and synthesizing the data we need to
advance biodiversity knowledge and conservation, we still have a long way to go when
it comes to understanding the diversity and distribution of EcM fungi in Canada. That
said, rapid progress is possible if we optimize future sampling and take advantage of
the lessons learned from other taxa and regions. To that end, our assessment marks an
important first step in improving our knowledge and understanding of EcM fungi in
Canada and provides the foundation for a coordinated, pan-Canadian sampling effort
that can be optimized to address multiple shortfalls simultaneously.

# Acknowledgements

This work was directly supported by a Canadian Institute for Ecology & Evolution Working Group grant (2025). We are grateful to the Accelerate Okanagan (Kelowna, BC) for providing collaboration space and logistical support. The research programs of JP, JK, and MF are supported by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada Discovery Grant program. SNK was funded by National Science Foundation awards 2106065, 2338421, 2412561. CQ was supported by a grant from the Paul G. Allen Family Foundation to the Society for the Protection of Underground Networks (SPUN).

# Data and code availability

Code for the entire workflow is publicly available (XXX). All input and output data
are archived and publicly available at (YYY). Supplemental Materials SM1 and SM2 are also archived at the same location.

# References

<!--
2026-07-06: all in-text citations checked against references.bib. Every
citation resolves to a bib entry via @key; no author-year prose citations
or unresolved gaps remain.
-->

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

# Tables

```{r}
#| include: false
# Compute the four table objects (t1-t4) and their caption text (cap_t1-cap_t4)
# separately from rendering. FACETS lists all table captions together on their
# own page, then presents each table alone on a following landscape page with
# no repeated caption - so the data/caption computation below is decoupled
# from where each piece is actually printed further down.

gf <- dplyr::filter(emf, source == "GlobalFungi")
gv <- function(m) as.numeric(V(gfv, m))   # GlobalFungi-wide volume metrics
cw <- function(m) as.numeric(V(gfw, m))    # GlobalFungi-wide "cheap" metrics
n_all_ca <- dplyr::n_distinct(
  readr::read_csv(paths$gf_meta_out, show_col_types = FALSE)$sample_ID)

can <- c(
  n_all_ca,                                                        # QF samples, all taxa
  dplyr::n_distinct(gf$sample_ID),                                 # QF samples with >=1 EcM
  if (!is.null(wall_loc)) wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "GlobalFungi"] else NA,
  sum(gf$abundance > 0, na.rm = TRUE),                             # detection records
  sum(gf$abundance, na.rm = TRUE),                                 # total read abundance
  dplyr::n_distinct(gf$species[named_ok(gf$species)]),             # named species
  dplyr::n_distinct(gf$genus[gf$genus != "" & !is.na(gf$genus)]),  # genera
  dplyr::n_distinct(gf$sh_code[gf$sh_code != "" & !is.na(gf$sh_code)]))  # SH codes
glob <- c(
  cw("GlobalFungi v5 samples worldwide (quality-filtered, all taxa)"),
  gv("GlobalFungi v5 samples worldwide (quality-filtered, with EcM detection)"),
  cw("EcM unique sampling locations (GlobalFungi-wide)"),
  gv("EcM detection records (GlobalFungi-wide, quality-filtered samples)"),
  gv("EcM total read abundance, Sigma reads (GlobalFungi-wide, quality-filtered samples)"),
  cw("EcM named species detected (GlobalFungi-wide, matrix presence)"),
  cw("EcM genera detected (GlobalFungi-wide, matrix presence)"),
  cw("EcM SH codes detected (GlobalFungi-wide, matrix presence)"))
t1 <- tibble::tibble(
  Category = c("Sampling effort", "Sampling effort", "Geographic coverage",
              "Detection volume", "Detection volume",
              "Taxonomic richness", "Taxonomic richness", "Taxonomic richness"),
  Metric = c("Quality-filtered samples, all fungal taxa",
             "Quality-filtered samples with $\\geq$ 1 EcM detection",
             "Unique EcM sampling locations",
             "EcM detection records (sample × SH-code, abundance > 0)",
             "EcM total read abundance (Σ reads)",
             "EcM named species detected",
             "EcM genera detected",
             "EcM SH codes (UNITE v10) detected"),
  Canada = nf(can),
  `GlobalFungi-wide` = nf(glob),
  `Canada as % of GlobalFungi-wide` = paste0(sprintf("%.1f", 100 * can / glob), "%")
)
cap_t1 <- "Summary of data derived from v5 of the GlobalFungi dataset. Summary data are provided for the Canada-specific samples, and for the full GlobalFungi dataset for comparison."

gb <- dplyr::filter(emf, source == "GenBank")
t2 <- tibble::tibble(
  Property = c("EcM fungal sequence records",
               "Records with coordinates",
               "EcM fungal SH codes",
               "SH codes with a UNITE species-level name",
               "EcM fungal genera",
               "EcM fungal named species",
               "Unique georeferenced sampling locations"),
  Value = nf(c(
    V(linn, "GenBank: total EcM sequence records"),
    if (!is.null(wall_loc)) wall_loc$n_records_with_coords[wall_loc$dataset == "GenBank"] else NA,
    V(linn, "GenBank: unique SH codes (Canadian dataset)"),
    V(linn, "GenBank: SH codes with species-level UNITE name"),
    dplyr::n_distinct(gb$genus[gb$genus != "" & !is.na(gb$genus)]),
    dplyr::n_distinct(gb$species[named_ok(gb$species)]),
    if (!is.null(wall_loc)) wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "GenBank"] else NA)))
cap_t2 <- "Properties of the Canadian GenBank EcM sequence dataset, after ITS2/ITS-both filtering and UNITE SH assignment ($\\geq$ 98.5% identity). Taxon counts are over all Canadian GenBank records."

pctc <- function(a, b) paste0(nf(a), " (", pr(a, b), "%)")
t3 <- tibble::tibble(
  `Taxonomic level` = c("Named species", "SH codes"),
  `Taxa` = nf(c(occ_sp$n, occ_sh$n)),
  `Q1 cells (Canada)` = nf(c(occ_sp$q1, occ_sh$q1)),
  `Median cells (Canada)` = nf(c(occ_sp$med, occ_sh$med)),
  `Q3 cells (Canada)` = nf(c(occ_sp$q3, occ_sh$q3)),
  `Max cells (Canada)` = nf(c(occ_sp$mx, occ_sh$mx)),
  `< 30 cells in Canada` = c(pctc(occ_sp$lt_can, occ_sp$n), pctc(occ_sh$lt_can, occ_sh$n)),
  `< 30 cells worldwide` = c(pctc(occ_sp$lt_glob, occ_sp$n), pctc(occ_sh$lt_glob, occ_sh$n)))
cap_t3 <- "Occupancy of georeferenced Canadian EcM fungal taxa (GlobalFungi only; sites defined as 30 arc-second grid cells). For each taxonomic level: number of taxa; the first quartile (Q1), median, third quartile (Q3), and maximum number of occupied cells within Canada; and the number (percentage) of taxa recorded in fewer than 30 cells within Canada and across the worldwide GlobalFungi v5 dataset."

if (!is.null(traits)) {
  t4 <- traits |>
    dplyr::transmute(Trait = dplyr::recode(trait_label, "Specific hosts" = "Host specificity"), `Trait class` = trait_class,
                     `Genera documented` = paste0(n_documented, " / ", n_total),
                     `Coverage (%)` = pct_documented)
  cap_t4 <- paste0("Trait coverage for the ", traits$n_total[1], " EcM fungal genera detected in Canada, across the six traits within the FungalTraits database applicable to EcM fungi. Coverage is the percentage of genera with a non-missing value; explicit “unknown” entries are counted as missing.")
} else {
  t4 <- NULL
  cap_t4 <- "raunkiaeran_trait_coverage.csv not found -- run 16_raunkiaeran.R."
}
```

**Table 1**: `r cap_t1`

**Table 2**: `r cap_t2`

**Table 3**: `r cap_t3`

**Table 4**: `r cap_t4`

```{=latex}
\begin{landscape}
```

**Table 1**

```{=latex}
\begin{singlespace}
```

```{r}
knitr::kable(t1, align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r"))
```

```{=latex}
\end{singlespace}
\end{landscape}
\begin{landscape}
```

**Table 2**

```{=latex}
\begin{singlespace}
```

```{r}
knitr::kable(t2, align = c("l", "r"))
```

```{=latex}
\end{singlespace}
\end{landscape}
\begin{landscape}
```

**Table 3**

```{=latex}
\begin{singlespace}
```

```{r}
knitr::kable(t3, align = c("l", rep("r", 7)))
```

```{=latex}
\end{singlespace}
\end{landscape}
\begin{landscape}
```

**Table 4**

```{=latex}
\begin{singlespace}
```

```{r}
if (!is.null(t4)) knitr::kable(t4) else
  cat("`raunkiaeran_trait_coverage.csv` not found — run 16_raunkiaeran.R.")
```

```{=latex}
\end{singlespace}
\end{landscape}
\newpage
```

# Figures

```{r}
#| include: false
# Compute figure caption text (cap_f1-cap_f5), decoupled from where each
# figure image is printed further down, for the same reason as the tables.
cap_f1 <- paste0("Sampling locations from which EcM fungal records were publicly available from either GlobalFungi v5 (n = ", nf(wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "GlobalFungi"]), " unique locations) or GenBank (n = ", nf(wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "GenBank"]), " unique locations), for a combined total of ", nf(wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "Combined"]), " unique locations. Many locations overlap so are not clearly visible.")
cap_f2 <- paste0("Frequency distributions of the number of 30 arc-second grid cells (~1 km²) occupied by EcM fungal taxa, GlobalFungi only. Top row: occupancy within Canada for (a) named species (n = ", nf(occ_sp$n), " species) and (b) SH codes (n = ", nf(occ_sh$n), " SH codes). Bottom row: occupancy across the full global GlobalFungi v5 dataset for the same (c) named species and (d) SH codes.")
cap_f3 <- paste0("Available climate space and Canadian EcM fungal sampling coverage, in geographic and climate space (WorldClim v2.1 BIO1 × BIO12, 50 × 50 grid of climate zones). Top row (a–c): geographic maps. Bottom row (d–f): the same in climate space. Left (a, d): available climate frequency; in (d), grid cells outlined in white indicate climates never sampled by GlobalFungi + GenBank combined, i.e. the grey zones in (f). Middle (b, e): GlobalFungi coverage; grey areas denote climates not sampled by any of the ", nf(wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "GlobalFungi"]), " GlobalFungi sampling locations. Right (c, f): combined GlobalFungi + GenBank coverage; grey areas denote climates not sampled by any of the ", nf(wall_loc$n_unique_locations[wall_loc$dataset == "Combined"]), " combined sampling locations. Figure style adapted from @hebertBlitzGapNationwide2026.")
cap_f4 <- paste0("Bivariate maps of EcM host species richness (BIEN2 modelled ranges) versus proportion of host species with EcM fungal molecular detection data in Canada, shown separately for non-tree (shrub and herbaceous; n = ", nf(n_nontree_mapped), " species; top) and tree (n = ", nf(n_tree_mapped), " species; bottom) EcM host species. Counts are the host species with a BIEN2 modelled range overlapping Canada, which is fewer than the full native host list (see Supplemental Materials SM1). Sampling unit: 0.5° grid cell; only cells with predicted EcM host habitat are shown.")
cap_f5 <- "Efforts to address current data shortfalls for EcM fungi in Canada can benefit from informed and optimized sampling programs that synergize progress to rapidly improve our taxonomic, geographic, and abiotic knowledge of EcM fungi across many host plant species. Ellipses show how sampling targeting a particular region (Wallacean) or abiotic suite of conditions (Hutchinsonian) can simultaneously address other data shortfalls, including Eltonian and Linnean. For example, the blue ellipse shows how sampling the under-represented climate conditions of the arctic (top-right quadrant) can simultaneously address the Linnean shortfall (top left quadrant). Likewise, the green ellipse shows how filling the large geographic sampling gap across the provinces of Manitoba and Saskatchewan (bottom left quadrant) could simultaneously address both the Hutchinsonian and Eltonian shortfalls (right quadrants)."
```

**Figure 1**: `r cap_f1`

**Figure 2**: `r cap_f2`

**Figure 3**: `r cap_f3`

**Figure 4**: `r cap_f4`

**Figure 5**: `r cap_f5`

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

Figure 1

```{r}
#| out-width: "85%"
if (file.exists(paths$fig_sampling_map)) knitr::include_graphics(paths$fig_sampling_map)
```

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

Figure 2

```{r}
#| out-width: "85%"
if (file.exists(paths$fig_wallacean_occ)) knitr::include_graphics(paths$fig_wallacean_occ)
```

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

Figure 3

```{r}
#| out-width: "95%"
if (file.exists(paths$fig_climate_gap)) knitr::include_graphics(paths$fig_climate_gap)
```

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

Figure 4

```{r}
#| out-width: "80%"
if (file.exists(paths$fig_host_bivariate)) knitr::include_graphics(paths$fig_host_bivariate)
```

```{=latex}
\newpage
```

Figure 5

```{r}
#| out-width: "95%"
if (file.exists(paths$fig_shortfalls_summary)) knitr::include_graphics(paths$fig_shortfalls_summary) else
  cat("*(This figure is a designed composite graphic; place it at figures/Figure-05_shortfalls_summary.png.)*")
```
